summary.intent: Address Codex review findings on PR #682 in fogodev/ars-ui by fixing meter/progress component edge cases related to invalid bounds, value sync, and ARIA attribute consistency, then verify CI passes.
summary.outcome: Four review findings were fixed (controlled progress value sync, meter percent/display bounds, progress percent invalid bounds, aria-valuenow consistency), tests were written and passed locally, and the fix was committed as f86c5f7e and pushed with all CI checks green.

repo: The ars-ui repo uses a Codex review bot triggered via @codex review PR comments; it signals review start with 👀 and opens threaded comments for findings.
CI pipeline has four jobs: Workspace Check, Clippy, Mutual Exclusion Guard, and Unit Tests — in roughly that order of completion speed.
Unit Tests is the longest-running CI job; previous workflow runs are auto-cancelled when new commits are pushed.
PR #682 is in the fogodev/ars-ui repo on branch t3code/f1fdd791.
The project uses cargo llvm-cov for coverage and proptest for property-based/state-machine tests alongside standard integration tests.
Review threads are resolved via GitHub GraphQL resolveReviewThread mutation using thread IDs.

code.finding: Meter percent must return 0.0 for invalid bounds before falling back to sanitized display bounds; threshold normalization was also incorrect.

Progress percent must return 0.0 for invalid bounds; aria-valuenow must be derived consistently with the invalid-bound percent logic; controlled progress events must preserve prop-driven value until parent syncs new props.
Integration tests for meter/stat/progress components live here; new red tests were added first (TDD) to guard the four review findings before patching implementation.

workflow: TDD approach was used: red failing tests written first to confirm they catch the intended bugs, then minimal implementation patches applied, then green tests verified.
Pre-push checklist: cargo fmt --all --check, cargo clippy with -D warnings, focused integration tests, module-level tests, proptest state machines, and llvm-cov summary.
Codex review threads require both a reply comment (referencing the fix commit SHA) and a GraphQL resolveReviewThread call to fully close them.
Polling CI state via 'gh pr checks' and 'gh run view' is more reliable than 'gh run list' alone for tracking individual job status.
Sleep-polling in 30–180 second increments was used to wait for CI and Codex review completion.

friction: Unit Tests job ran significantly longer than other CI jobs, requiring multiple extended polling cycles (up to 180s sleep intervals) before completing.

Codex review reaction state (👀 → ✅/❌) is not available via standard REST reactions endpoint in a granular way, requiring cross-referencing reactions list with unresolved threads to infer review completion.
Multiple sleep-poll loops were needed because there is no push-based notification for CI completion or Codex finishing its review.
open_items: No open items identified — all four review findings were addressed, tests passed, and the commit was pushed. The next pending action is waiting for Codex to complete its follow-up review pass on the new commit.
